Task 1 — Pipeline Architecture Diagram
1.1 End-to-End Architecture Diagram

The following architecture describes the complete data pipeline designed for the online retail system. The pipeline processes both historical batch data and real-time transaction events. Data flows through ingestion, validation, transformation, and layered storage before becoming available to downstream consumers such as business dashboards and machine learning models.

This architecture separates responsibilities into clear stages so that data quality can be monitored, failures can be isolated, and datasets can be safely reprocessed when pipeline logic changes.

┌──────────────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────────────┐
│ Historical Batch Source      │         │ Live Transaction Stream      │
│ Online Retail.xlsx dataset   │         │ New transaction events       │
└──────────────┬───────────────┘         └──────────────┬───────────────┘
               │                                        │
               ▼                                        ▼
┌──────────────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────────────┐
│ Batch Ingestion              │         │ Stream Ingestion             │
│ Reads Excel file and         │         │ Captures row-by-row events   │
│ converts rows to records     │         │ with ingestion metadata      │
└──────────────┬───────────────┘         └──────────────┬───────────────┘
               └──────────────────────┬─────────────────┘
                                      ▼
                     ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
                     │ Raw / Landing Zone               │
                     │ Append-only source-faithful      │
                     │ storage for all ingested records │
                     └──────────────┬───────────────────┘
                                    ▼
                     ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
                     │ Validation Layer                 │
                     │ Schema, value, and business      │
                     │ rule checks                      │
                     └──────────────┬───────────────────┘
                          valid     │         invalid
                                    │
                    ┌───────────────┘───────────────┐
                    ▼                               ▼
     ┌──────────────────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────────────────┐
     │ Transformation Layer         │   │ Quarantine / Dead Letter     │
     │ Cleaning, enrichment,        │   │ Failed records + error logs  │
     │ derived columns              │   └──────────────┬───────────────┘
     └──────────────┬───────────────┘                  ▼
                    ▼                    ┌──────────────────────────────┐
     ┌──────────────────────────────┐   │ Monitoring & Alerting        │
     │ Clean Layer                  │   │ Data quality & pipeline logs │
     │ Validated transaction data   │   └──────────────────────────────┘
     └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                    ▼
     ┌──────────────────────────────┐
     │ Feature Layer                │
     │ Aggregated customer features │
     │ and ML-ready datasets        │
     └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                    ├──────────────────────────┐
                    ▼                          ▼
     ┌──────────────────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────────────────┐
     │ BI Dashboard                 │   │ ML Training Pipeline         │
     │ Sales analytics and reports  │   │ High-value customer model    │
     └──────────────────────────────┘   └──────────────────────────────┘


1.2 Describe Each Component
Batch File

The historical batch file acts as the initial source of data for the pipeline. It contains the full Online Retail.xlsx dataset, which includes transaction records such as invoice numbers, product identifiers, quantities, prices, timestamps, and country information. This dataset is loaded once during the initial pipeline setup to populate the historical dataset used for analytics and feature generation. The input is the Excel dataset itself, and the output is a sequence of transaction records that are passed into the batch ingestion stage.

Live Transaction Stream

The live transaction stream represents real-time purchase events generated by the retailer’s operational systems. Each event corresponds to a single transaction and is typically represented as a structured JSON message. These events are continuously captured by the streaming ingestion system. The output of this stage is a continuous stream of raw transaction events entering the pipeline for further processing.

Batch Ingestion

The batch ingestion component reads the historical Excel dataset and converts each row into a structured transaction record. Its primary purpose is to reliably load the entire historical dataset into the pipeline without applying transformations. The output is a set of raw records written into the raw landing zone together with ingestion metadata such as ingestion timestamp and pipeline run identifier.

Stream Ingestion

The stream ingestion component captures incoming transaction events as they occur. These events are recorded with minimal processing so the original data can be preserved. Each record receives ingestion metadata such as timestamp and event identifier. The resulting records are written to the raw storage layer for validation and further processing.

Raw / Landing Zone

The raw storage layer stores incoming data exactly as it was received from the ingestion systems. This layer serves as the immutable source of truth for the pipeline. Both batch and streaming records are written in append-only format together with metadata describing the ingestion time and source. Keeping the raw data unchanged ensures that the pipeline can reprocess historical data if validation or transformation logic changes.

Validation Layer

The validation layer checks each incoming record against predefined schema rules, value constraints, and business logic. It ensures that required fields are present, data types are correct, and values fall within acceptable ranges. Valid records proceed to the transformation stage, while invalid records are redirected to the quarantine dataset. This step prevents corrupted or logically inconsistent data from entering downstream analytical datasets.

Quarantine / Dead Letter Area

The quarantine area stores records that fail validation checks. Each failed record is stored together with metadata describing the validation rule that failed and the reason for rejection. This dataset allows data engineers to inspect problematic records and investigate upstream data issues. Once issues are corrected, quarantined records can be reprocessed through the validation stage.

Transformation Layer

The transformation layer converts validated raw transactions into analysis-ready datasets. This stage performs operations such as cleaning values, computing derived columns (for example line totals), extracting date features, and generating aggregated metrics. The output of this stage is a structured dataset ready for analytical workloads and feature generation.

Clean Layer

The clean layer stores validated and transformed transaction data in a standardized format. This dataset contains consistent data types, normalized values, and derived columns that support analytics and reporting. Because analytical workloads frequently scan large datasets, a columnar storage format such as Parquet is appropriate for efficient storage and query performance.

Feature Layer

The feature layer stores aggregated datasets derived from the clean transactional data. These datasets include customer-level metrics such as total spending, purchase frequency, product diversity, and recency indicators. Feature datasets are used by both analytics dashboards and machine learning pipelines.

BI Dashboard

The BI dashboard consumes aggregated datasets to produce visual reports and business insights. These dashboards display metrics such as daily sales totals, top-selling products, and country-level revenue distribution. Dashboards are typically refreshed on a nightly schedule using batch updates.

ML Training Pipeline

The machine learning pipeline uses customer-level features generated by the pipeline to train predictive models. In this scenario, the model predicts whether a customer is likely to become a high-value customer. Feature datasets are refreshed daily, while the machine learning model is retrained on a weekly schedule.

Monitoring and Alerting

The monitoring system tracks the health of the entire pipeline. It checks data freshness, record volumes, schema consistency, and statistical distributions. Alerts are triggered when anomalies are detected, such as missing data batches, sudden drops in transaction volume, or unexpected schema changes. Monitoring ensures that data quality problems are detected quickly before affecting analytics or machine learning workflows.



Task 2 — Validation and Error Handling Design
2.1 Validation Rules

To ensure data quality and prevent corrupted records from entering downstream storage layers, the pipeline includes a comprehensive validation stage. The validation layer checks incoming records using three categories of rules: schema validation, value range validation, and business rule validation.

These checks guarantee that the data is structurally correct, logically consistent, and aligned with the retailer’s operational processes. If a record fails any validation rule, it is not allowed to enter the clean processing layer and is instead redirected to a quarantine dataset for investigation.

Schema Validation (Structural Correctness)

Schema validation ensures that incoming records contain the expected fields and that each field has the correct data type. These checks prevent malformed records from entering the pipeline.

| Field       | Expected Type | Required | Validation Rule                    |
| ----------- | ------------- | -------- | ---------------------------------- |
| InvoiceNo   | String        | Yes      | Must be a non-empty string         |
| StockCode   | String        | Yes      | Must be a non-empty string         |
| Description | String        | No       | If present, must be a valid string |
| Quantity    | Integer       | Yes      | Must be a numeric integer          |
| InvoiceDate | Datetime      | Yes      | Must be a valid timestamp          |
| UnitPrice   | Float         | Yes      | Must be a numeric value            |
| CustomerID  | Numeric       | No       | Must be numeric if present         |
| Country     | String        | Yes      | Must contain a valid country name  |


These rules ensure that the pipeline processes only structurally valid records and prevents incorrect data types or missing required fields from propagating further into the system.

Value Range Validation (Sensible Values)

Value range validation ensures that numeric fields fall within realistic and logically consistent ranges.

| Field       | Validation Rule                         |
| ----------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Quantity    | Must not be zero                        |
| Quantity    | Absolute value must be less than 10,000 |
| UnitPrice   | Must be greater than or equal to 0      |
| UnitPrice   | Must not exceed 10,000                  |
| InvoiceDate | Cannot be in the future                 |
| CustomerID  | Must be positive if present             |


These rules help detect corrupted records, application errors, or extreme outliers that could distort analytical results.

Business Rule Validation (Domain Logic)

Business rule validation checks logical relationships between fields that reflect the retailer’s operational processes.


| Rule                     | Description                                                                                                     |
| ------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Cancellation rule        | If `InvoiceNo` begins with **C**, then `Quantity` must be negative                                              |
| Non-cancellation rule    | If `InvoiceNo` does not begin with **C**, then `Quantity` must be positive                                      |
| Revenue consistency      | `Quantity × UnitPrice` must produce a valid transaction total                                                   |
| Product identifier rule  | `StockCode` must not be empty                                                                                   |
| Customer feature rule    | Transactions without `CustomerID` remain in the dataset but are excluded from customer-level feature generation |
| Duplicate detection rule | Duplicate transactions with the same `InvoiceNo`, `StockCode`, and `InvoiceDate` should be flagged              |



These rules ensure that transactions follow the expected business logic of the retail system and prevent logically inconsistent data from affecting downstream analytics.

2.2 Error Handling Flow

When validation failures occur, the pipeline must handle them systematically rather than silently discarding problematic records. The error handling process includes record rejection, quarantine storage, monitoring, and recovery procedures.

Handling Schema Validation Failures

Schema validation failures occur when required fields are missing or contain incorrect data types.

Handling strategy:

The invalid record is rejected from the main pipeline flow.

The record is written to a quarantine dataset together with metadata describing the validation error.

The pipeline logs the failure event and increments a schema validation error counter.

If the failure rate exceeds a predefined threshold, the monitoring system triggers an alert.

This approach prevents structurally invalid records from corrupting downstream datasets while still preserving them for debugging and correction.

Handling Value Range Failures

Value range failures occur when numeric values fall outside acceptable boundaries.

Handling strategy:

The record is written to a dead letter dataset together with the rule that failed.

Error metrics such as the affected field and invalid value are logged.

Monitoring dashboards track error frequency over time.

Alerts are triggered if unusual spikes in invalid values are detected.

These checks help engineers quickly detect upstream system bugs or incorrect application inputs.

Handling Business Rule Violations

Business rule violations indicate logical inconsistencies between fields.

Handling strategy:

The failing record is written to the quarantine dataset with a business-rule failure label.

The pipeline continues processing valid records normally.

Detailed logs record the violated rule and associated field values.

Alerts notify operators if rule violations occur frequently.

This ensures that logical inconsistencies do not propagate into analytics datasets or machine learning features.

Quarantine Storage and Recovery

All rejected records are stored in a dedicated quarantine storage area together with structured metadata.

Each quarantined record includes:

Original raw transaction record

Validation error category (schema, value range, or business rule)

Specific validation rule that failed

Timestamp of the failure

Pipeline run identifier

This structure allows engineers to inspect errors, identify root causes, and repair upstream data issues.

Once the issue is fixed, quarantined records can be reprocessed by sending them back through the validation stage. Because the pipeline transformations are idempotent, reprocessing the same records will not produce duplicate outputs.

Monitoring and Alert Integration

Validation metrics are integrated into the pipeline’s monitoring system so abnormal conditions can be detected quickly.

Alerts may be triggered when:

Validation failure rates exceed expected thresholds

A sudden spike occurs in schema or value errors

Daily ingestion batches are missing

Transaction volume drops unexpectedly

Schema drift is detected in incoming data

By integrating validation metrics into monitoring systems, the pipeline ensures that data quality issues are detected early and resolved before affecting downstream analytics or machine learning workflows.


Task 3 — Transformation and Storage Design
3.1 Data Transformations

After records pass the validation stage, the pipeline performs a series of transformations to convert raw transactional data into structured datasets suitable for analytics and machine learning. These transformations include data cleaning, derived columns, customer-level aggregations, and feature engineering.

All transformations are designed to be idempotent, meaning that running the same transformation multiple times on the same input will always produce the same result. This property ensures that the pipeline can safely reprocess historical data without creating duplicates or inconsistencies.

Cleaning Transformations

Cleaning transformations standardize and normalize records that passed validation but still require formatting or deduplication before analysis.


| Transformation                | Input                         | Output                      | Idempotent |
| ----------------------------- | ----------------------------- | --------------------------- | ---------- |
| Standardize timestamp format  | Raw `InvoiceDate` values      | Parsed datetime field       | Yes        |
| Remove duplicate transactions | Validated transaction records | Unique transaction dataset  | Yes        |
| Normalize country names       | Country field values          | Standardized country values | Yes        |
| Handle missing descriptions   | Description field             | Cleaned or null-safe values | Yes        |


These transformations ensure that transaction records are consistent and ready for downstream analytical workloads.

Derived Columns

Derived columns enrich transaction-level records with useful calculated attributes that simplify downstream analytics.

| Transformation             | Input                   | Output                              | Idempotent |
| -------------------------- | ----------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ---------- |
| Line total calculation     | `Quantity`, `UnitPrice` | `line_total = Quantity × UnitPrice` | Yes        |
| Cancellation flag          | `InvoiceNo`             | Boolean `is_cancellation` field     | Yes        |
| Date feature extraction    | `InvoiceDate`           | `date`, `hour`, `weekday` columns   | Yes        |
| Transaction value category | `line_total`            | Categorized transaction segment     | Yes        |


These derived fields reduce the need for repeated calculations in reporting and analytics queries.

Customer-Level Aggregations

Customer-level aggregations summarize historical transactions into behavioral metrics that describe customer purchasing patterns.

| Transformation         | Input                     | Output                             | Idempotent |
| ---------------------- | ------------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ---------- |
| Total customer revenue | Customer transactions     | Sum of `line_total` per customer   | Yes        |
| Order count            | Customer transactions     | Number of orders per customer      | Yes        |
| Product diversity      | Customer purchases        | Count of unique `StockCode` values | Yes        |
| Recency calculation    | Customer purchase history | Days since last purchase           | Yes        |


These metrics are useful for both business analytics and machine learning feature generation.

Feature Engineering for Machine Learning

The pipeline also generates customer-level features used by the machine learning model that predicts high-value customers.

| Transformation            | Input                                        | Output                                | Idempotent |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------- | ---------- |
| Build feature snapshot    | Clean transactions within observation window | One row per customer with ML features | Yes        |
| Average order value       | Customer purchase history                    | `avg_order_value` feature             | Yes        |
| Purchase frequency        | Customer transactions in time window         | `purchase_count` feature              | Yes        |
| Product diversity feature | Customer purchase history                    | `unique_products_purchased`           | Yes        |
| Recency feature           | Last purchase timestamp                      | `recency_days` feature                | Yes        |

To avoid data leakage, these features are calculated only using transactions that occur before the observation cutoff date used for model training.

3.2 Storage Layer Design

The pipeline follows a layered storage architecture consisting of raw, clean, and feature datasets. Each layer serves a different purpose and supports different workloads.

| Layer   | Contents                                        | Format  | Update Frequency     | Retention   |
| ------- | ----------------------------------------------- | ------- | -------------------- | ----------- |
| Raw     | Original ingestion records with metadata        | Parquet | Continuous ingestion | Long-term   |
| Clean   | Validated and standardized transaction data     | Parquet | Daily batch updates  | Long-term   |
| Feature | Customer-level features and aggregated datasets | Parquet | Daily refresh        | Medium-term |


Raw Layer

The raw layer stores all ingested data exactly as it was received from upstream sources. This layer acts as the immutable source of truth for the pipeline. Each record includes ingestion metadata such as source identifier, ingestion timestamp, and pipeline run identifier.

Although the historical dataset originates as an Excel file and streaming data may arrive as JSON events, the raw layer stores an append-only representation in Parquet format. This allows efficient storage and partitioned access while preserving the original record content.

Maintaining a raw dataset allows the pipeline to reprocess historical data if validation rules or transformation logic change in the future.

Clean Layer

The clean layer stores validated and standardized transactional data after transformation. This dataset contains normalized values, consistent data types, and derived columns such as transaction totals and cancellation flags.

The clean layer is the primary dataset used for analytical queries, reporting, and feature generation. Because analytics workloads frequently scan large datasets but use only a subset of columns, a columnar format such as Parquet is well suited for efficient query performance.

Feature Layer

The feature layer stores aggregated datasets derived from the clean transactional data. These datasets include customer-level metrics used by machine learning models as well as summary tables used by business dashboards.

Examples of feature datasets include:

Customer revenue metrics

Purchase frequency indicators

Product diversity metrics

Daily sales summary tables

Top product performance metrics

Feature datasets are refreshed on a scheduled basis rather than continuously. Daily updates provide fresh data for analytics while allowing machine learning models to retrain periodically.

3.3 Incremental Update Strategy

Production data pipelines must process new data incrementally rather than recomputing the entire dataset each time the pipeline runs. This pipeline uses a combination of high-water mark tracking, sliding reprocessing windows, and scheduled feature refreshes.

Tracking Processed Data

The pipeline maintains a high-water mark that represents the timestamp of the most recently processed transaction. During each pipeline run, only records with timestamps greater than the stored high-water mark are processed.

This mechanism prevents duplicate processing and ensures that only newly arrived records are processed during each execution.

Pipeline metadata such as processed timestamps and run identifiers are stored in a checkpoint table to track pipeline progress.

Handling Late-Arriving Data

In real-world systems, some transaction events may arrive later than expected due to upstream delays or network issues.

To handle this scenario, the pipeline uses a sliding reprocessing window. For example, each daily pipeline run may reprocess transactions from the previous few days. This ensures that delayed records are incorporated into the dataset without requiring full historical recomputation.

Duplicate detection logic ensures that reprocessed records do not produce duplicate outputs.

Feature Refresh Strategy

Customer-level features must remain up-to-date to support both analytics and machine learning.

The pipeline refreshes feature datasets daily using the latest clean transaction data. These updates ensure that business dashboards always reflect recent activity.

The machine learning model is retrained weekly using historical feature snapshots produced by the pipeline. Separating feature refresh from model retraining ensures fresh data while controlling computational cost and maintaining reproducibility.


Data Lineage Strategy

To ensure transparency and traceability across the pipeline, the system maintains lineage information for every dataset that is produced. Data lineage allows engineers and analysts to track how data moves through the pipeline, from the original sources to the final datasets consumed by analytics dashboards and machine learning models.

Each pipeline run records metadata that describes the origin and transformation history of the data. This metadata includes information such as the source dataset or event stream, the ingestion timestamp, the pipeline run identifier, and the version of the transformation logic applied during processing.

The lineage metadata enables engineers to trace records in the clean layer and feature layer back to their corresponding entries in the raw storage layer. If a data quality issue or unexpected analytical result occurs, engineers can identify which raw inputs and transformation steps produced the affected data.

Maintaining lineage also improves debugging, auditing, and reproducibility. Because every pipeline run records its input sources and transformation versions, historical datasets can be reproduced if needed. This is especially important for machine learning pipelines, where training data must remain traceable to ensure reliable model evaluation and explainability.

In addition, lineage tracking helps detect upstream data issues. If errors or anomalies appear in downstream datasets, the lineage metadata allows engineers to quickly locate the original source of the problem and determine which stage of the pipeline introduced it.
